sentences.text: Good morning.
OK.
My full name is Max Joseph Wunderman.
I'm born the 18th of April, 1930 in Antwerp, Belgium.
OK.
I have my father, Nathan Wunderman, my mother, Hena Wunderman, a sister, Bella Wunderman, and a brother, Sevirin Wunderman.
My father was working as a-- how do you call this-- a weight control.
He was checking when the ship came in.
He was checking what the content was.
And also, he was an expert in grains.
He worked for a company.
He was employed by this-- the port from Antwerp.
And my mother, she had a tailor shop in Brussels.
And my father worked with her, too-- when he came home and he worked with her.
The children-- we were all going to school.
Because my sister is a couple years older than me, and my brother was a baby.
So that's about all.
Yes.
I didn't go too much to school because I went to school to the age of about 11 years old.
But I went to primary school.
And the school was in Brussels, Rue de [FRENCH].
School number four.
And this I went to the age of 11.
And then we couldn't go to school anymore, because the Germans took away the rights of the Jewish kids to go to school.
So then we were sent away to camps with other children.
We were in hiding so that nobody-- do you want me to tell you about this?
Well, until you had to leave the school, what was it like for Jewish children in the school?
Do you have memories of that?
Well, it was a difference.
Because to begin with, all the Jewish kids were wearing a yellow star.
So we-- me in particular-- I had a teacher that was a terrible anti-Semite.
He made a big, big difference.
He put the Jewish children on the side in one class not to mingle with the other kids.
So that really made a difference as much as he could.
Because for schooling, they couldn't stop us to go to school, because there was no law against it.
But you could feel the resentment.
Not between the other children so much but the teacher himself.
Because this particular teacher after the war, they put him to jail anyhow.
And I went to testify against him, this particular teacher.
He was a member of the German group, I don't know.
Can you remember what he said, the kinds of things he said that were anti-Semitic?
He said that on the account of the Jews that they had so much problem in the world.
And I don't remember that much.
But you could feel it-- the resentment that he had.
The Jews were not clean.
The Jews were people that took advantage from other people.
And all kinds of things.
How much can I remember?
You know, it's not-- but in his way, he was against us for sure.
That's all.
Well, it's like I live here in the United States.
I lived in a community with a lot of Jews around us.
So we didn't really feel.
Because the Belgian people themselves didn't care for the Germans at all.
So there were more friends to us than they were against us.
So we didn't feel really strange.
Still, the Germans really got after us and started to deport the Jews.
But till '41, it was more or less a normal life, except the Jews couldn't go on the streets after 8 o'clock at night.
And they stopped-- arrested, harassed the people.
They made them problems and all kinds of things.
You know, to make their lives miserable.
And then like in the other countries probably, first they start taking all the men away, the head of the families, and down the line.
See?
That's the way it worked.
Do you remember as a child any celebrations you had-- holidays?
How did you celebrate?
Well, I personally-- like I said, till 1941, I lived a normal life.
Because life went down as a Jewish community.
And I belonged to a Jewish school.
And I went to Sunday school and celebrate the holidays like the Jewish people do.
But I'm not-- I was never too religious, so we celebrate Hanukkah and things like this, you see.
Also, if you want to know, I was in the Jewish theater when I was a kid.
And I was quite busy with that.
Because before the war in '38, we had a big group going on.
And we supposed to come to America to represent us.
And then the war started, and the whole thing got canceled.
But the school went down till '41, and then everything got fell apart.
But I belonged to the Jewish theater for quite a while then.
Yes.
The main role that I had Motl the [YIDDISH] from Shalom Aleichem.
And I was the little boy, a very mischief boy.
The father dies in there, and he takes over the whole milk.
He sells the milk to the people.
And then he goes and plays.
And I remember he spills the milk.
So he takes soap water, he puts it in, and he goes and sells to the woman the milk with water and soap.
It was very funny.
But it was a nice piece of work from Shalom Aleichem mainly.
It was fun.
Yiddish, yeah.
I spoke good Yiddish, but I couldn't read or write.
So my mother was reading to me, and I had to remember everything by memory.
But I did pretty good.
And then I was going to school to learn how to read.
But in the beginning, I couldn't read the Yiddish.
So everything by memory.
But it was nice.
This I remember was the good years, that's for sure.
No, I never met my grandparents.
They were alive but in Poland.
And I never went back to Poland.
My mom went back once in '35, I think, and took my sister.
I didn't want to go, so I stayed with my dad.
But I never went to Poland.
Well, in '41 because the Belgian underground contacted the Jewish family and said that they're rounding up-- they're starting to round up the Jews.
And they don't want that the all Jewish children should disappear.
They cannot protect every adult, but at least let them protect the children.
So whoever wanted to, they gave away their children.
But you had to sign a paper that you couldn't see them or anything for the safety of the children.
So my parents gave us away, all three of us.
And this was in 1941.
And from '41 to '44, I never saw them anymore, my parents.
Describe to me how it-- you know, the whole way that it happened.
Who came to talk to you?
How it happened.
The first time I went to the school.
I stayed there a couple of months.
And then, we didn't go back to-- we didn't go back home.
We went back to a home it called-- in Brussels it was.
[FRENCH], J-O-C, the Youth of Catholic Children.
And they took care.
The Catholic people took care of the Jewish children.
They made a group of us.
And we stayed there for a couple days in Brussels.
And then the priest came.
His name was [FRENCH] Renard.
I remember very well.
And he came and picked up about-- we must have been in my group there about 20 children, Jewish children.
And he took us.
And he took us to our first home.
And the first home was Jamoigne.
This was an old, old converted castle that made a home for children-- not just Jewish children, but poor children.
And they integrated a lot of Jewish childrens with it in Jamoigne.
And this was the director-- they know about it-- and the Holocaust survivors, because his name was Major Tacquet.
I showed you the calendar.
And his wife.
They were the main directors from that.
And it was organized like a scout-- like scout camp.
They had boys and girls.
The girls were separate but mainly boys.
And we were like troops from Boy Scouts.
And I stayed there for quite a while, for quite a while.
Till-- should I go on with that?
Sure.
Till again-- you know how boys are.
They don't always behave.
So were a group of about 10 kids.
The bigger ones-- because we knew that we were Jewish.
The little ones were no problem.
They listen.
But the bigger ones-- they go and put us to go to church and stuff like this.
It just was almost impossible because we didn't believe in that.
We made a mockery.
So it was a friction.
No.
One of the teachers, OK, that took care of us.
He resented us.
Again, he was against us really, because he was losing his authority.
So he made us all kind of problems, even fist fights, so bad it was with this guy.
Till finally, we didn't behave the way we should.
We got punished one after the other and one after the other.
And finally, they throw us out.
The 10 kids we were.
They throw us in the middle of the war in 1942, maybe '43.
They took this group and they say, we don't want those troublemakers.
And they kicked us out.
So this poor priest, [FRENCH] Renard-- he came back, and he picked us all up and back to Brussels in this camp-- in this for the children.
We stayed there a few days.
And then we went away, and we went to Bastogne.
This was a seminary.
They didn't know what to do with us in the meantime, so they put us for two months in a seminary.
That's supposed to be that we were punished.
But it turned out to be a blessing in disguise, because we had all the food that we needed.
For once in my lifetime, I was eating until content.
Because all those years we were barely surviving.
That there wasn't enough food, you know?
And if you are a child that develops himself, you need food.
And we just didn't have it.
So in this, we did everything possible to get more.
But best thing there in Bastogne was great, and we stayed there for about two months.
And then back to Brussels until they decide again what to do with us.
And then we went to Cul-des-Sarts.
Cul-des-Sarts was again a camp for children like this, for underprivileged children.
And we are mixed with Jews.
But I would say, 70% of the kids there were Jewish in Cul-des-Sarts.
Also an old castle that was worked over for a day camp for children.
And there I stayed at least a year, a year and a half.
And there we were going to school.
The under-- the Belgian underground was taking care and bringing money.
For-- because my parents paid for us.
Every month they sent money.
But whoever ran out of money-- because during the war, they didn't work-- my parents were hiding during the war.
They didn't work at all.
So we sold everything we had just to keep on making payments, because we were three kids in the family.
And with some people that didn't have the money it didn't make a difference, because the underground was helping us.
They were taking banks during the war, and robbery-- by all means, to support the children.
It was a big undertaking.
No, separated.
We never-- I never even knew where they were.
I didn't know where my sister was.
I didn't know where my brother was.
I only find out after, because I wasn't even in touch with my parents.
I could not write to them.
They didn't want that the parents should know where the children were.
So I just didn't know.
Period.
Except maybe-- no, I'm not telling the truth.
Because I got a couple letters through somebody that knew my parents.
So my parents wrote to them, and he put it in an envelope and sent it to us.
Maybe one or two letters I got during the war.
That's about all.
And I knew-- I knew that my sister was hiding someplace else and my brother was.
But a personal touch we didn't have at all.
What did your parents tell you when they first-- you know, when you were first going to be taken to the homes?
They told us that they deport us if we stay.
If they're going to catch us, to go away.
At least, us, we will survive.
That somebody should remain from the family.
That's why they did the sacrifice, and gave away the children.
They didn't have them in mind.
They had the safety of us.
And as a matter of fact, in account of this, most of the children in Belgium survived.
The parents deported and killed but not the children.
They did a big job for the children.
A lot of them remained orphans.
I was lucky my parents survived.
But not all of them were like this.
Most of it if you want to think back, it's like normal children do.
We didn't do any-- we didn't do any robbery or killing or anything, but we were not disciplined the way it should be.
Those guys-- the chiefs of the scout-- if they say stand up, we should stand up.
Sit down, sit down.
We had to do so many chores.
Every time we did something wrong, we had to go-- I remember I had to peel a mountain of potatoes.
I was-- I didn't see the sun in front of my eyes.
I was peeling potatoes all day long.
They came in with shovel coal.
We had to do.
Always the same group, the same bunch of guys.
But we were the bigger ones.
See, I was already-- I was pretty big for my age.
When I was 12 years old, I was the size that I'm now-- I mean, no.
Maybe not-- but when I was about 14 years old.
And then I didn't grow no more.
So I was a big kid in that time.
So I got all those things to do.
And I was following the other ones.
What do you think?
I wasn't by myself like this.
That's the mischief we made.
But we didn't do really, really bad.
Except we didn't listen to what we were supposed to do and made troubles with the other kids, you know.
But we didn't do it really that bad.
Because the other kids still stayed there, so it could not have been that bad.
For us, it was a good thing.
It was a good thing that we went away.
Because they got peace, and we went to a place where we liked more.
It's as simple as that.
But I thought it was part of my life to go in those children's camps, because I didn't know anything better.
That's the way it was.
The main problem that we had is they wanted to convert the little children to Catholicism.
And by the fact that we knew that we were Jewish, we were fighting against this.
We were telling who they are, and what their heritage is.
This was the main conflict.
Because they were against the German-- the people.
But mainly, to save a Jewish soul and make a Catholic.
This was an accomplishment.
So not that they meant bad by that, but we resented that.
Because like I said, we knew our heritage, and we tried to say this to the little kids.
So what we did-- we each took in charge one or two little children that was really follow us around.
And we were teaching them who you are and what to do, and that's why they resented us, you see-- mainly.
That's why they had to get rid of us, because they couldn't use their influence.
So they really had like a court between all the leaders from there, and then they decided to expel us.
But it's already so far away.
You forget.
But it's part of my youth.
What can I say?
And in the next places that you went to, did you have to go to church?
What kind of--
Well, in the seminary, we had to go because everybody was going.
But we didn't take the communion or anything like this.
But I did go to church.
I know pretty good about Catholicism, for sure.
They teach me.
But I took what was good for me out of it and that was it.
But there I didn't stay too long.
I stayed a couple of months.
And then I went to Cul-des-Sarts.
That's where I went like a normal child.
I went to school during the day, and playing normal activities, and all kind of things that we did over there.
Because the main thing was no food.
Very little food.
So we had to organize ourselves.
What do I mean by organized?
We went and we steal, period.
All kind of stuff to survive.
That's why the kids that came out of there they were tough cookies, I'll tell you one thing.
Because most of them were without parents.
And when they came back after the war, all my friends-- I was one-- maybe one out of 10 that had parents.
The rest were deported.
Nobody came back.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is past stories, that's all.
And where were your parents during this time?
My parents were in Brussels all this time.
What they did, they-- a friend of theirs rented a house under his name.
And my parents were making the payments through this man, and they were hiding there.
My mom never went out, because she was with dark hair, and she looked more like a Jewish lady.
My father didn't look like a Jew at all, because he had light blue eyes and curly blondish hair, brownish hair.
So he went around, and he was dressed with overalls.
He looked like a plumber or something with a box of tools.
And he went like he would go to work and walked around.
And went to do the shopping and things like this.
He was very lucky, because he went to-- and he spoke good language.
That's very important.
Without any accent or anything like that.
He spoke Flemish, my father.
Because in Brussels you speak Flemish and French.
But he spoke perfect Flemish.
And he got caught once during the war.
That they-- should I talk to you about it?
There was one guy in Belgium.
His name was Jacques.
And in 1942, he started working for the Germans.
He was fingering all the Jews.
And that's the way they started picking up Jews-- through him.
They were putting Jews-- because he knew a lot of people.
The guy was a doorman from a big hotel in Brussels.
And he was a Jew?
He was a Jew.
I know his first name.
His name was Jacques, and we used to call him Jacques the [NON-ENGLISH].
People from Belgium will know, tell you about it.
They know him.
A traitor.
Yes.
And the first people that he sent away to camp was his own wife and his own daughter.
The very first ones-- to get rid of his wife and kid.
And then he got into a lot of people.
Till the end of the war, this guy.
And then the last transport that went away from Belgium, the German himself took him, too.
Sent him to camp.
And the Jews in camp killed him.
This I know for a fact, because I had a friend that was in the camp where he came in.
They put him in a blanket, and they all stomped on him.
They just killed him.
That was it.
They make a pulp out of it.
He got what he deserved-- period.
Maybe not enough yet.
But this I remember very well.
My parents, they survived.
And immediately after the war, when the war was over, my father hitchhiked with the American army.
And they came into the camp where we were to come and get me, because he was afraid that I'm going to hook up with the army and start going, too.
So he-- in-- at the end of '44.
Because the Americans came in and cooked up or something like this in Brussels.
So my father came to get us.
And then I went back to Brussels, and I stayed with my dad.
But that was three long years, that's for sure.
Did you have a false name, false papers?
Yes.
My name was Marcel Barre, B-A-R-R-E.
This was the name of this person that rented the house from my parents.
I took his identity.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah.
This was my name during the war.
What else would you like to know?
No.
Absolutely not.
But between the children, we didn't even tell.
Because they told us-- the underground told us-- your name is Marcel Barre.
Whenever they ask, that's it.
Don't reveal your name, because you may put your parents in danger.
So we knew.
I was already old enough to understand this, you see.
So I went under this one name and stayed like this.
We had identity papers, and we had false identities.
And we had because we used to get stamps during the war, you know, for food stamps.
So that's the way we collected it, through this false name.
But I-- paperwork-- I didn't have nothing to do, because I was very well sheltered in this camp for children in Cul-des-Sarts.
It still exists, you know.
I went to see it when I went back to Belgium.
I went to visit the old grounds.
For me when I was a kid, it looked so huge and everything.
And when I came back now, it looked so small.
[LAUGHS] It's a different perspective, you know.
Well, when we were-- one event that really stands out for us is-- this was in the-- no, in Cul-des-Sarts.
Yeah.
An American plane got shot down, and he flew over the castle and crashed.
So immediately, the people pulled out the pilot.
They didn't-- they were not even wounded.
They pulled him out, and hauled him away for safety.
But then the Germans came in looking for them, you see.
And they came in to the-- the came into the camp where the childrens were and started looking for the American soldiers.
So I remember distinctly that they were looking in our beds to see if we didn't hide them.
And the children that looked too Jewish-- right away, they took them away for the whole day in the woods that we shouldn't see each other.
Because they were afraid that they will make one plus one is two.
But they didn't look-- they didn't pay attention to the kids.
They were really looking for the American fliers.
But they got away, this I know.
Yes.
They put me to the woods.
I had dark black hair, you know.
The ones with blond hair and stuff they remained there.
And the other ones, they took us away.
And we went into the woods for the whole day, I remember.
You know, a child thinks very differently.
It's part of a game.
Because we didn't know better.
I really never suffered from the Germans, so I didn't really think it's that bad.
And I didn't-- how did I-- I couldn't understand that they kill us over there if they catch us, you know.
So we went along what they told us to do, you see.
But we didn't mean that bad-- for us it wasn't that bad.
It's part of a game.
Because a child's mentality is different than another.
I was maybe very mature for my age, but I still was not an adult, you know.
So that's the way we survived.
That's all.
What about friends, girlfriends?
I mean, did boys and girls get together during that time?
Very much so.
Very much so.
Because if you get mature faster for your age in one way, you get mature for another way, too.
So boys and girls-- and there was plenty of girls available.
And that's a normal thing.
Children are like this, and that's about all.
I don't have nothing to talk in particular, but I have nothing to be ashamed of either.
[LAUGHS] So it doesn't make a difference.
But we got along.
I have good seven years from it.
All kind of adventures that happened to us.
OK.
We had a friend over there.
Not a Jew, a goyem from the village.
I forgot the name.
But he had a factory from cigars.
The father, he was making little cigars.
So we went-- a bunch of guys of us.
And we were wearing those golf pants, you know, that go down there.
So we went there, and while they were not looking, we filled up our pants with cigars.
[LAUGHS] Then with this we came back, and then we went to the peasants and we exchanged cigars for eggs, for butter, for bread.
And we bought a dinner.
We distributed for all the kids.
I'm telling you, we organized ourselves.
The bigger kids, we were very, very well organized.
We used to go to the butcher, and we could buy meat without the stamps-- black market.
But it used to cost maybe 10 times more than the regular way.
So we came in and we said, we want to have, let's see-- for $1 a small piece of meat on the black market.
But the guy didn't have the meat the same place that he had the other, so he had to go in the back to get this other meat.
While he went in other the back, we went and we stole stamps.
And when he came back, we say, we changed our mind.
We don't want this.
Maybe we're going to buy with stamps, and we bought for cheaper.
OK.
